reason: Options are single-word punctuation-mark names; expanding would break option-set parallelism.

rewrites.newText: She glided through the pool as smoothly as a darting silver fish.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: simile mistaken for personification
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This is a simile comparing a person to a fish; nothing non-human is given human qualities.

rewrites.newText: The overgrown back garden was a lush green paradise of tangled colour.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: metaphor mistaken for personification
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This is a metaphor; the garden is given no human action or feeling.

rewrites.newText: I was so embarrassed that I very nearly died of shame on the spot.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: hyperbole mistaken for personification
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This is hyperbole describing a human speaker; no non-human thing is given human traits.

rewrites.newText: A single word that names a person, a place, an object, or a feeling.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: clause confused with a single-word noun
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This defines a noun; a clause is a group of words built around a subject and verb.

rewrites.newText: A punctuation mark you place at the end of a sentence to close it.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: clause confused with punctuation (a full stop)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This describes a full stop; a clause is a grammatical group of words.

rewrites.newText: A complete sentence that expresses a whole idea and can stand entirely on its own.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: phrase confused with a full sentence
rewrites.wrongnessReason: A phrase lacks a subject-verb pair; this describes a full sentence.

rewrites.newText: A literary device, such as a simile or metaphor, that a writer uses to create imagery.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: 'phrase' confused with a figure of speech
rewrites.wrongnessReason: A grammatical phrase is a structural word group, not a figurative technique.

rewrites.newText: Simply another word for a clause, since both must always contain a subject and a matching main verb.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: phrase confused with a clause
rewrites.wrongnessReason: A phrase has NO subject-verb pair, so it cannot be another word for a clause.

rewrites.newText: An unusually long and complicated word that is difficult to spell correctly.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: contraction confused with a long word
rewrites.wrongnessReason: A contraction is a shortened form joining two words with an apostrophe.

rewrites.newText: A describing word that tells you more about a noun in a sentence.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: contraction confused with an adjective
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This defines an adjective; a contraction is a shortened word form.

rewrites.newText: To show that something belongs to a particular person or thing.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: ellipsis confused with a possessive apostrophe
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Possession is shown by an apostrophe, not an ellipsis.

rewrites.newText: To introduce a list of items before you name each one.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: ellipsis confused with a colon
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Introducing a list is the job of a colon.

rewrites.newText: To bring a sentence to a normal, complete stop like any full stop.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: ellipsis confused with a full stop
rewrites.wrongnessReason: An ellipsis deliberately leaves a sentence unfinished; a full stop ends it completely.

rewrites.newText: To add strong emphasis to an important word or idea.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: speech marks confused with emphasis marking
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Emphasis is shown with italics or bold; speech marks enclose spoken words.

rewrites.newText: To join two shorter sentences into one longer sentence.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: speech marks confused with a joining word
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Joining sentences is done by conjunctions or semi-colons, not speech marks.

rewrites.newText: To mark the exact words that a person speaks aloud.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: semi-colon confused with speech marks
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Marking spoken words is the job of inverted commas; a semi-colon joins two clauses.

rewrites.newText: To show that something belongs to a named person or object.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: semi-colon confused with a possessive apostrophe
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Possession is shown with an apostrophe, not a semi-colon.

rewrites.newText: To build suspense by letting a sentence trail off into silence.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: semi-colon confused with an ellipsis
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Trailing off is done with an ellipsis; a semi-colon joins two complete statements.

rewrites.newText: Repeating the same word several times to create a strong effect.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: onomatopoeia confused with repetition
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This describes repetition; onomatopoeia is a word whose sound imitates a noise.

rewrites.newText: A wild overstatement that stretches the truth far beyond what is real.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: onomatopoeia confused with hyperbole
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This defines hyperbole; onomatopoeia is about a word's sound imitating a noise.

rewrites.newText: Comparing two different things using the words 'like' or 'as'.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: personification confused with simile
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This defines a simile; personification gives human qualities to non-human things.

rewrites.newText: Saying that one thing actually is another, without using 'like'.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: personification confused with metaphor
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This defines a metaphor; personification gives human traits to non-human things.

rewrites.newText: An over-the-top exaggeration used purely for dramatic effect and comic emphasis.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: personification confused with hyperbole
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This defines hyperbole; it involves no giving of human qualities.

rewrites.newText: A comparison between two things using the linking word 'as'.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: hyperbole confused with simile
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This defines a simile; hyperbole is exaggeration and makes no comparison.

rewrites.newText: Giving human feelings and human actions to lifeless objects or animals.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: hyperbole confused with personification
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This defines personification; hyperbole is exaggeration.

rewrites.newText: Yes — both words mean exactly the same, so the two sentences say the same thing.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: assuming a shared root means a shared meaning
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'Hard' and 'hardly' have opposite meanings, so the sentences do not say the same thing.

rewrites.newText: 'Hardly' is not a proper word at all, so only the first sentence makes real sense.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: believing 'hardly' is not a real word
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'Hardly' is a genuine adverb meaning almost not at all.

rewrites.newText: Yes — 'hardly' is simply a more formal or polite way of saying the very same thing.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: treating the difference as one of register, not meaning
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The two words differ in meaning, not formality.

reason: Options must be exact verbatim fragments of a short quoted stimulus sentence; no genuine part is long enough to hit the word targets.

rewrites.newText: Please open the heavy wooden door very quietly now.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: a longer sentence assumed to have two clauses
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This is a single imperative clause with one verb ('open').

rewrites.newText: She quickly ate her entire packed lunch in the busy playground.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: added detail mistaken for a second clause
rewrites.wrongnessReason: There is only one verb ('ate') and one subject, so this is a single clause.

rewrites.newText: The beautiful summer garden bloomed with bright red and yellow flowers.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: coordinated adjectives mistaken for two clauses
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The 'and' joins two colour adjectives, not two clauses; one verb ('bloomed'), one clause.

rewrites.newText: Main clauses are always longer than the subordinate clauses beside them.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: distinguishing clauses by length rather than independence
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The distinction is that a main clause makes sense alone; length is irrelevant.

rewrites.newText: There is really no difference; the two terms mean the same thing.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: believing the two terms are synonyms
rewrites.wrongnessReason: A main clause stands alone, a subordinate clause cannot, so there is a clear difference.

reason: Options must be verbatim clause fragments of a quoted stimulus; explanationQuotesThis is true for all three and no genuine fragment reaches the word targets.

rewrites.newText: Because it describes what the noun is actually like, like an adjective.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: treating 'the' as describing the noun (adjective rationale)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'The' specifies which one, it does not describe what a noun is like.

rewrites.newText: Because it replaces a noun completely, working just like a pronoun does.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: confusing 'the' with a pronoun
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'The' introduces a noun rather than standing in for it.

rewrites.newText: Noun — it names the thing that got damaged here.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: mislabelling the participle as a noun
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'Record' is the noun; 'broken' is the past participle of 'break'.

rewrites.newText: Adverb — it tells you how the verb 'was' happened here.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: mislabelling the participle as an adverb
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'Broken' does not modify 'was'; it is the past participle forming the passive verb.

rewrites.newText: Adjective — it simply describes what the record is like, nothing more.
rewrites.targetsMisreading: reading a passive participle as a describing adjective
rewrites.wrongnessReason: With 'yesterday' marking a completed event, 'was broken' is a passive verb, not an adjective.
